stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 with an MCDA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 26 weeks. Booking, anatomy scan, and TTTS surveillance to date have been unremarkable. Today the smaller twin has an EFW on the 4th centile with a 28 percent inter-twin weight discordance. The larger twin tracks the 48th centile. Amniotic fluid is normal in both sacs. Umbilical artery Doppler of the smaller twin shows intermittently absent end-diastolic flow that alternates with normal forward flow within a single tracing; the larger twin's UA Doppler is normal. Both ductus venosus a-waves are positive. The cardiotocograph of both twins is reactive. The woman is well with a closed cervix of 34 mm. The pregnancy was spontaneously conceived; she has had no episodes of vaginal bleeding, no rupture of membranes, no contractions and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. Booking BMI was 24, booking bloods were unremarkable and she has had no infections during the pregnancy. The local maternity unit has a tertiary neonatal service on site with experienced obstetric and foetal-medicine input available daily. She has had no abdominal pain and no recent infections of any kind.
lead_in: Which classification and surveillance plan best fits this pattern?

options.text: Type I sIUGR; fortnightly growth scans, aim for planned delivery at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Type I has persistently positive UA end-diastolic flow with good prognosis; the intermittent AEDF here is Type III, not Type I, per Gratacos classification.

options.text: Type II sIUGR; weekly Doppler and CTG surveillance, plan delivery at 32 weeks
options.rationale: Type II has persistently absent or reversed UA flow with progressive deterioration; the intermittent pattern here is Type III.

options.text: Type III sIUGR; twice-weekly Doppler with corticosteroid cover and delivery at 32 to 34 weeks
options.rationale: Intermittent AEDF in the smaller MCDA twin defines Type III, which carries unpredictable risk of sudden co-twin demise from large anastomoses; close surveillance and planned preterm delivery are recommended.

options.text: Discordant growth without sIUGR; reassure the woman and re-scan in 2 weeks
options.rationale: EFW under the 10th centile in either twin with discordance over 25 percent meets the consensus definition of sIUGR, so this is not benign discordance.

options.text: Stage I twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome; refer urgently for fetoscopic laser ablation now
options.rationale: TTTS requires discordant fluid (DVP under 2 and over 8 cm); fluid is normal in both sacs here, so this is sIUGR rather than TTTS.

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 presents to the birth suite with regular painful contractions every 4 minutes for the past 2 hours. She has had no bleeding, no rupture of membranes, and no fever. Her booking and pregnancy have been uncomplicated. She has well-controlled chronic hypertension on labetalol 200 mg BD with booking BP averaging 130/82 and today 134/86. She has known mild aortic stenosis (peak gradient 22 mmHg, asymptomatic) and is followed by cardiology. Examination shows a soft tender uterus with palpable tightenings, cephalic presentation, and the cervix is 2 cm dilated and 50 percent effaced on speculum. Foetal heart is reassuring. Foetal fibronectin is not available at this hour. The plan is to give corticosteroids and MgSO4 for neuroprotection and transfer to a tertiary unit. She has had no antepartum haemorrhage and her booking infection screen was unremarkable. The neonatal team at the tertiary unit has confirmed cot availability; estimated transfer time by road is 90 minutes. Indomethacin and prostaglandin synthetase inhibitors are stocked at the local unit, as are atosiban and nifedipine. She has no contraindication to magnesium sulfate or to any tocolytic listed.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate tocolytic to commence while corticosteroids and transfer are organised?

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release as calcium-channel blocker tocolysis now
options.rationale: Nifedipine is a calcium channel blocker; combining with antihypertensives such as labetalol increases the risk of severe maternal hypotension and is relatively contraindicated per Therapeutic Guidelines.

options.text: Intravenous atosiban infusion as oxytocin-receptor antagonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: Atosiban is the preferred tocolytic when there are maternal cardiovascular concerns, is effective and has a favourable maternal safety profile vs nifedipine, and avoids the hypotensive interaction with antihypertensives.

options.text: Oral indomethacin 50 mg loading dose then 25 mg six-hourly
options.rationale: Indomethacin is generally avoided after 32 weeks (ductus arteriosus constriction, oligohydramnios) and is not first-line; at 28+3 it can be considered but is not preferred over atosiban here.

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion used primarily as tocolysis
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is not recommended as a tocolytic; it is used for foetal neuroprotection at gestations under 30 weeks (RANZCOG).

options.text: Intravenous salbutamol infusion as beta-2 agonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: Beta-agonists are no longer recommended for tocolysis in Australia because of maternal cardiovascular and metabolic adverse effects.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 31+5 presents with threatened preterm labour. She had a complete course of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart at 26+0 for an earlier admission with threatened preterm labour that subsequently settled. Today she is contracting every 3 minutes, has a cervix 3 cm dilated and 70 percent effaced on speculum, and a foetal fibronectin from 4 weeks ago was positive. The CTG is reactive. The team plans to start tocolysis, transfer to a tertiary unit, and give magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection. She has no other comorbidities. She has no rupture of membranes, no bleeding, no fever and no offensive vaginal discharge. Her observations are normal and a recent maternal infection screen is unremarkable. The tertiary unit she is being transferred to has confirmed cot availability; estimated road transfer time is 75 minutes. The team is making a decision about further antenatal corticosteroid dosing, aware that her first course finished more than 7 days ago and that the cervix is now actively dilating. She has no other obstetric complication at present and her foetal observations remain reassuring.
lead_in: Regarding antenatal corticosteroids, which is most appropriate now?

options.text: Give a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM now
options.rationale: RANZCOG supports a single rescue dose when the initial course was given over 7 days ago, gestation is under 34+0, and birth is anticipated within the next 7 days.

options.text: Give a full repeat course of betamethasone (two doses 24 h apart)
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends a single rescue dose rather than a full repeat course; multiple repeats are associated with reduced birth weight.

options.text: Do not give further corticosteroids as one course is sufficient lifetime exposure
options.rationale: A single rescue dose is supported when birth is now imminent and the prior course was over 7 days ago; outright omission misses meaningful neonatal benefit.

options.text: Give dexamethasone 6 mg IM as a single rescue dose
options.rationale: The dose of dexamethasone in Australian practice is 12 mg IM per dose, not 6 mg; a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM is preferred per RANZCOG.

options.text: Withhold steroids until cervix is more than 5 cm dilated
options.rationale: Steroids are given before imminent birth, not delayed until advanced dilation, because the foetal benefit takes hours to develop.

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 28+2 presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid 2 hours ago, followed by ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, no abdominal tenderness, and the foetal heart is reassuring. Speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix and the cervix appears closed. There is no offensive odour. Maternal observations are normal. Her GBS status from a 26-week swab is negative. She is admitted for expectant management with corticosteroids, MgSO4, and close maternal-foetal surveillance. She has no abdominal pain, no contractions, no foetal heart abnormality on ongoing CTG and no offensive vaginal discharge. The amniotic fluid pool is clear and not bloodstained. Maternal infection screen and FBC are unremarkable, and she has no allergies to penicillins or macrolides. The plan is for inpatient expectant management to at least 34 weeks if she remains stable, with corticosteroids, magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection and antibiotic prophylaxis to prolong latency in keeping with current RANZCOG and Royal Women's PPROM guidance. She has no contractions and no signs of clinical chorioamnionitis on serial review. Erythromycin and amoxicillin-clavulanate are both stocked at the local maternity unit.
lead_in: Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate to prolong latency?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the ORACLE I evidence support oral erythromycin in PPROM to prolong latency and reduce neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased necrotising enterocolitis risk.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is associated with an increased rate of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis in the ORACLE I trial and is not recommended for PPROM.

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g six-hourly for 48 hours of treatment only
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin is GBS intrapartum prophylaxis, not a latency regimen; alone it does not provide the prolongation seen with erythromycin.

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g as a single stat dose for latency prolongation
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is used as adjunct prophylaxis at caesarean in some settings, not as the latency regimen of choice for PPROM.

options.text: No antibiotic prophylaxis unless overt chorioamnionitis later develops in this patient
options.rationale: Latency antibiotics are routinely given in PPROM under 34 weeks to prolong pregnancy and reduce neonatal infection independent of clinical chorioamnionitis.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 36+5 presents 3 hours after a gush of clear fluid. She has no contractions, the foetal heart is reassuring, and she is afebrile. Speculum confirms PROM with a pool of clear fluid; the cervix appears favourable. She is GBS positive on a 36-week swab. Her last birth was a spontaneous vaginal birth at term without complication. She is otherwise well and has no allergies. The unit's neonatal services routinely accept babies from 35 weeks. She has no contractions, no abdominal pain, no fever and the foetal heart is reassuring on continuous CTG. Maternal observations and a recent FBC are unremarkable. She has no penicillin allergy. The neonatal unit on site routinely accepts babies from 35 weeks and there is no logistic barrier to immediate induction of labour. The team is weighing immediate induction against short-term expectant management in the setting of PPROM beyond 36 weeks with confirmed group B streptococcal colonisation on her recent vaginal-rectal swab. She has been counselled about both immediate induction and short-term expectant management options. Induction of labour and intrapartum GBS prophylaxis can be commenced today.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Plan induction of labour now with intrapartum benzylpenicillin for GBS
options.rationale: PPROM at or beyond 36+0 with a GBS-positive mother is managed by induction rather than expectant care to limit chorioamnionitis and neonatal early-onset GBS sepsis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Expectant management at home for 96 hours with daily review
options.rationale: Outpatient expectant management at near-term PPROM with GBS positivity is unsafe due to early-onset neonatal sepsis risk and inability to start timely intrapartum prophylaxis.

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine and a course of corticosteroids
options.rationale: At 36+5 corticosteroids are not routinely indicated and tocolysis would prolong rupture of membranes without benefit while increasing infection risk.

options.text: Caesarean section now due to PPROM beyond 36 weeks
options.rationale: PPROM at this gestation is not itself an indication for caesarean; vaginal birth after induction is preferred when there are no other contraindications.

options.text: Expectant management as an inpatient until 39+0 weeks
options.rationale: Delaying birth to 39 weeks with PPROM and GBS positivity is contrary to RANZCOG guidance and significantly raises infectious morbidity.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 attends preconception clinic having recently had a third painless mid-trimester loss at 19 weeks. The two prior losses were at 18 and 20 weeks; on each occasion she presented with bulging membranes and minimal contractions, and the cervix was widely dilated on speculum. Anatomy scans had been normal, there was no antepartum bleeding, and she has had no infections or instrumentation between pregnancies. She has no chronic medical conditions and takes only folate and iodine. Her last cervical screening is up to date and normal. She is keen to conceive again and asks how the next pregnancy will be managed. A detailed review of each loss documented painless cervical dilation as the dominant clinical feature, with rapid progression to delivery once membranes were exposed. There was no antepartum haemorrhage, no abruption, no clinical chorioamnionitis and no documented infection at the time of any of the losses. She has had no cervical surgery or LLETZ, no congenital uterine anomaly on previous imaging and no connective tissue disease. She has been counselled about the recurrence risk after three painless mid-trimester losses.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for her next pregnancy?

options.text: History-indicated cervical cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Three or more prior second-trimester losses or preterm births consistent with cervical insufficiency is the classic indication for history-indicated (elective) cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks per RANZCOG.

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly from 16 weeks without cerclage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is offered for short cervix on TVS but is not the standard for women with three prior second-trimester losses; cerclage is preferred in this history.

options.text: Serial cervical length scans from 16 weeks with rescue cerclage if shortening
options.rationale: Ultrasound-indicated (and rescue) cerclage are reasonable for women with one prior preterm birth, not three losses; elective cerclage outperforms watchful waiting here.

options.text: Pre-conception transabdominal cervical cerclage placement before next pregnancy attempt
options.rationale: Trans-abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or anatomical contraindication to vaginal cerclage, not first-line.

options.text: Insertion of cervical pessary at 14 to 16 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Pessary evidence is mixed and not first-line in Australia for women with this history; transvaginal cerclage is the standard.

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 at 37+1 with well-controlled gestational diabetes on diet alone is reviewed at her routine antenatal clinic. Foetal growth is on the 50th centile, amniotic fluid is normal, CTG is reactive, and self-monitored capillary glucose has stayed within target. She has no hypertension, no IUGR, and no other complication. She has booked into a private hospital and asks whether elective induction at 37 to 38 weeks would be safer than waiting. She has no history of stillbirth or previous large-for-gestational-age infant, and no obstetric or medical indication for early delivery. Her ADIPS-aligned OGTT thresholds were borderline at booking but glycaemic control has been excellent on diet alone, with all fasting glucose values below 5.1 mmol/L and post-prandial values below 7.0 mmol/L. The unit follows ADIPS and RANZCOG guidance which does not recommend routine early delivery before 39 weeks in well-controlled diet-managed GDM with normal foetal surveillance. She has been counselled about the lack of evidence supporting routine early delivery in well-controlled diet-managed GDM. She has been counselled about RANZCOG and ADIPS timing recommendations. Her partner is supportive of expectant management.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care with planned delivery around 40+0 to 40+6 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS and RANZCOG support birth by around 40+6 for diet-controlled GDM with no complications; routine early term induction does not improve outcomes and increases neonatal respiratory morbidity.

options.text: Elective induction of labour at 37+0 weeks gestation now
options.rationale: Routine induction at 37+0 in well-controlled diet-only GDM is not recommended; it increases iatrogenic late preterm morbidity without clear benefit.

options.text: Elective lower segment caesarean section at 38+0 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Elective caesarean is not indicated in well-controlled diet-only GDM without other obstetric reasons.

options.text: Planned induction at 39+0 with cervical ripening regardless of Bishop
options.rationale: Insulin-treated or complicated GDM may warrant earlier induction; for diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM, ADIPS supports expectant management to around 40+6.

options.text: Defer delivery until 41+5 weeks with daily CTG from 39 weeks
options.rationale: Even uncomplicated GDM is generally not allowed to progress beyond 40+6; deferring to 41+5 increases stillbirth risk above the accepted threshold.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 29+0 is on day 9 of expectant PPROM management. She completed corticosteroids 7 days ago and erythromycin is ongoing. She has been afebrile with normal observations until this evening, when she reports increased uterine tenderness and chills. Observations: T 38.4, HR 118, BP 108/64, RR 18, SpO2 98 on RA. Her uterus is tender on palpation. CTG shows a baseline foetal heart rate of 175 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. WCC has risen to 18.6 x10^9/L, neutrophils 16.2 x10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L (from 18 mg/L two days ago). She has no offensive vaginal discharge but the speculum view shows turbid amniotic fluid pooling. Maternal lactate is 2.1 mmol/L. There are no other localising features of infection. She has no flank pain, no dysuria, no respiratory symptoms and no cellulitic or wound features on examination. A FAST scan of the abdomen is unremarkable and her venous blood gas shows pH 7.36, lactate 2.1 mmol/L, base excess -2 and normal electrolytes. The CTG over the past 30 minutes has been persistent foetal tachycardia at 170 to 180 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. Her tertiary unit has a paediatric retrieval team on standby. The team is balancing the risks of continued expectancy at this previable-leaning gestation against the strong clinical picture of clinical chorioamnionitis and early sepsis with foetal compromise.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and expedite delivery now
options.rationale: Clinical chorioamnionitis in PPROM at 29 weeks is an indication for prompt delivery alongside IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, irrespective of gestation, per RANZCOG.

options.text: Continue expectant management with IV antibiotics, aim to reach 32 weeks
options.rationale: Expectant management is contraindicated once chorioamnionitis is established because of maternal sepsis and foetal injury risk; prolonging pregnancy is unsafe.

options.text: Repeat antenatal steroids and reassess clinical status in 12 hours
options.rationale: Delaying delivery for steroids during overt intra-amniotic infection raises sepsis risk; the foetus is already affected and steroids will not change the trajectory in time.

options.text: Add tocolysis to allow safe transfer to a tertiary unit
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in chorioamnionitis because it delays definitive treatment (delivery) and masks deterioration.

options.text: Amniocentesis for Gram stain and glucose before any decision
options.rationale: With clear clinical chorioamnionitis (fever, maternal and foetal tachycardia, tender uterus, rising CRP, turbid AF), delaying for amniocentesis is unnecessary and unsafe.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with an MCDA twin pregnancy at 27+4 presents with reduced foetal movements over 24 hours. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated; serial scans have shown concordant growth and no TTTS. Today's ultrasound confirms intrauterine demise of twin A with absent cardiac activity and small pericardial effusion. Twin B has a foetal heart rate of 148 bpm with reactive CTG, normal amniotic fluid, normal umbilical artery Doppler, and a peak systolic velocity in the middle cerebral artery of 1.8 multiples of the median. Maternal observations and coagulation profile are normal. She has no contractions and the cervix is 35 mm closed on TVS. She has had no vaginal bleeding and no rupture of membranes. Her platelet count, INR, APTT and fibrinogen are within normal limits and a repeat coagulation profile in 6 hours is planned. The MCA peak systolic velocity of 1.8 multiples of the median in the surviving twin exceeds the 1.5 MoM threshold widely used to suggest moderate to severe foetal anaemia, raising concern for an acute feto-foetal transfusion event at the time of co-twin demise in this monochorionic pregnancy. The tertiary foetal medicine service two hours away offers ultrasound-guided intrauterine transfusion of the surviving twin with experienced operators available on a same-day basis. She has been counselled on the risks of acute feto-foetal transfusion at co-twin demise.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Urgent transfer to foetal medicine for assessment for foetal anaemia
options.rationale: MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM in the surviving twin after MC co-twin demise suggests acute foetal anaemia from feto-foetal transfusion across shared anastomoses; intrauterine transfusion may reduce neurological injury (RANZCOG, ISUOG).

options.text: Immediate lower segment caesarean section of the surviving twin at this gestation
options.rationale: Iatrogenic birth at 27+4 inflicts severe prematurity morbidity and does not reverse hypotensive injury already sustained; intrauterine resuscitation is preferred first.

options.text: Expectant management with weekly outpatient Doppler surveillance of the surviving twin
options.rationale: MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM is a red flag for foetal anaemia and requires urgent tertiary assessment; weekly outpatient surveillance is too slow.

options.text: Commence maternal intravenous heparin to prevent disseminated intravascular coagulation in mother
options.rationale: Maternal coagulopathy after single twin demise is rare in the short term; this woman has normal coagulation and prophylactic heparin is not indicated.

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the surviving twin by ultrasound-guided cord occlusion
options.rationale: Termination of the surviving healthy twin is not appropriate; the goal is to assess and rescue the survivor.

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman, 4 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, was diagnosed with right-breast bacterial mastitis 5 days ago and commenced flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily with continued feeding and expression. Her fevers settled within 48 hours but the erythematous area has now developed a discrete, fluctuant tender lump over the past 24 hours despite ongoing antibiotics and effective drainage. She is afebrile today, haemodynamically stable, and the skin overlying the lump is intact, non-necrotic and not under tension. Bedside ultrasound performed by the GP demonstrates a well-defined, unilocular hypoechoic collection measuring 38 mm x 24 mm at 1.5 cm depth, with no overlying skin necrosis. She has no recurrent abscess history, no immunosuppression, no diabetes and wishes to continue breastfeeding. She is comfortable being managed in primary care if the lesion is amenable to office-based drainage and the local Breast Centre offers ultrasound-guided procedures on a same-day basis. She has no recent travel, no recent skin lesions and no flu-like systemic symptoms. The Breast Centre has confirmed availability for ultrasound-guided aspiration the same afternoon if requested. She has been counselled about office-based ultrasound-guided aspiration as the preferred first-line drainage option.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current oral flucloxacillin and review in 5 days without drainage
options.rationale: A drained collection requires drainage to resolve; antibiotics alone are inadequate and risk chronicity, fistula and lactiferous duct injury (ABM Clinical Protocol 36).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with culture; continue antibiotics, reaspirate if needed
options.rationale: For a lactational abscess under approximately 5 cm with intact non-necrotic skin, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration (often serial) with cultured aspirate and continued antibiotics is first-line; it preserves duct architecture, allows ongoing breastfeeding and avoids a surgical wound that complicates feeding (ABM; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Refer for theatre incision and drainage with packing under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Surgical I&D is reserved for very large abscesses, multiloculated collections, or where the skin is thinned/necrotic; it leaves a wound that may interfere with feeding and is unnecessary for a 4 cm unilocular collection (ABM).

options.text: Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast and continue antibiotics only
options.rationale: Cessation worsens milk stasis and slows resolution; current guidance is to continue feeding from both breasts where the infant will latch, or to express if the infant refuses (ABA).

options.text: Switch to oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily and observe without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic broadening without drainage is inadequate; an established collection needs source control (ABM).
